title: Secrets in CI/CD: NIST and FedRAMP alignment
summary: Maps plaintext-secret anti-patterns to NIST AC/IA families and FedRAMP access-control expectations.
body:
Credentials embedded in repository YAML defeat rotation, auditing, and least privilege. NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5 controls AC-2 (Account Management), AC-3 (Access Enforcement), IA-5 (Authenticator Management), and SC-12/SC-13 (Cryptographic Key Establishment and Protection) collectively expect secrets to be provisioned through approved mechanisms—not checked into VCS.

FedRAMP baselines inherit these controls; Moderate environments typically require multifactor integration with enterprise IdP and vault-backed secrets for CI. Replace inline password: or token: literals with GitLab CI/CD variables (masked, protected) or a supported secrets manager.

steps: Rotate any exposed credential; treat the repository as compromised until verified.
Move values to masked CI variables or vault references; restrict branch/environment scope.
Add server-side scanning (secret detection) in merge requests.
remediation.verification: Grep for password:/token: patterns in .gitlab-ci.yml; confirm only variable references remain.

nist.name: Account Management
nist.discussion: Accounts for non-person entities (CI jobs) should be provisioned and reviewed.

nist.name: Authenticator Management
nist.discussion: Authenticator distribution must not rely on cleartext storage in repos.

nist.name: Cryptographic Key Establishment and Management
nist.discussion: Keys and tokens require protected establishment paths.

fedramp.notes: Document CI service accounts and vault integration in the SSP.
FedRAMP Moderate: align secret lifecycle with organizational key management policy.
